preFix.title: Polymer clay sculpted fox
preFix.subtitle: A sitting fox figurine in orange, white, and black polymer clay with blended colour boundaries
preFix.excerpt: A sitting fox figurine approximately 60 mm tall, built from a teardrop body with a separate muzzle, ear slabs, and a coiled tail. Orange, white, and black polymer clay are blended at colour boundaries for a naturalistic coat. Cured in stages in a domestic oven, with glass bead eyes and a satin varnish finish.

content.text: This sitting fox is built from five main components: a teardrop body, a wedge muzzle, two pointed ear slabs, and a coil tail. An
armature
of crumpled aluminium foil in the body reduces the clay mass, which speeds curing and reduces the risk of cracking on a 60 mm form. Colour
blending
at the boundaries between orange, white, and black zones produces the natural gradation of a fox coat. No kiln required: polymer clay hardens in a domestic oven at 110 to 130 degrees C.

tools.name: Wooden rolling pin or acrylic roller

content.text: Preparation

Warm all three clay colours by kneading each separately for one to two minutes. Keep hands clean between colours to prevent cross-contamination at the boundaries. Prepare a small crumpled ball of aluminium foil approximately 35 mm across to act as the body armature. You will also need two small glass or polymer beads approximately 3 mm in diameter for the eyes, and satin varnish for the finish. Pre-heat the oven before each curing session.
Construction
Body
Compress the foil ball into a teardrop shape approximately 35 mm tall and 25 mm wide at the base. This is the
armature
. Wrap it in a thin layer of orange clay approximately 3 mm thick, pressing the clay firmly against the foil and smoothing all seams.

The narrow top of the teardrop will become the upper chest and neck. The wider base is the haunches where the fox sits. Press the base flat against the work surface so the figure sits stably without rocking.

Add the white belly area. Roll a thin sheet of white clay and press it onto the front of the body, from the base to about the midpoint. Stroke along the join line with a fingertip to blend the edge into the orange. This gives a soft colour change, not a hard line.
Blend
the edges of the white patch into the orange by pressing and stroking with a fingertip along the join line, creating a gradual colour transition rather than a sharp edge.
Head and face
Roll a ball of orange clay approximately 22 mm in diameter for the head. Press it onto the top of the body and smooth the neck join all the way round.

Build the muzzle: take a small wedge of orange clay approximately 12 mm wide and 8 mm tall. Press it onto the front lower half of the head, centred horizontally. The muzzle should protrude approximately 5 mm from the face plane. Blend the edges into the head surface.
Add white clay to the front of the muzzle and blend at the edges for the pale muzzle colouring typical of foxes. Add a tiny black ball for the nose at the tip of the muzzle.

Position the eye beads: press two glass or polymer beads into the head approximately 6 to 7 mm apart and 10 mm from the top of the muzzle. Use the needle tool to create a seat for each bead and press them in firmly so they are half submerged in the clay.

Build the ears: cut two pointed triangles from a thin slab of orange clay approximately 15 mm tall and 10 mm wide at the base, 3 mm thick. Line the inner face of each ear with a thin patch of white clay. Press the ears onto the top of the head approximately 8 mm apart, angled very slightly outward.
Add black clay in a strip from the outer base of each ear down the side of the nose to the outer muzzle. Blend the edges. This creates the characteristic black facial markings of a red fox.
Tail and front paws

Roll a thick coil of orange clay approximately 60 mm long and 12 mm in diameter, tapering toward one end. This is the tail. Curve it into a crescent shape and press the thick end against the right side of the body base, curving around behind the figure so the tip rests at the left front.

Blend the tail join into the body. Add a patch of white clay to the tail tip and blend the edges for the white-tipped tail. A few strokes with the needle tool along the tail surface suggest fur texture.

Roll two small logs of black clay approximately 10 mm long and 5 mm in diameter for the front paws. Press them against the front base of the body, one on each side of the tail tip. Blend the top of each paw into the body.
Curing
Place the completed fox on a ceramic tile and
cure

at 110 to 130 degrees C for 40 minutes. The foil armature in the body means heat penetrates to the core more efficiently than in a solid clay mass, but allow the extra time for the full body depth. Allow to cool completely on the tile before handling.
Finishing

Sanding is optional for a sculptural figurine: a matte surface reads more naturally as fur than a polished one. If the surface has fingerprints or tool marks, smooth them with a fingertip before curing. After curing, apply one coat of satin varnish over the entire figure to unify the surface sheen and protect the piece. Two coats of gloss varnish can be applied to the eyes and nose only to give them a bright wet appearance.
Care

This figurine is not food-safe and is for display only. Do not place in a microwave or dishwasher. Dust with a soft brush. Avoid dropping: polymer clay figurines at this size are durable but thin elements like ear tips can snap under impact. Store away from direct sunlight, which can cause colour fading over years.
Variations
Arctic fox: use white clay with pale blue-grey shadowing; swap out orange for entirely white with light blue ear interiors.
Lying-down pose: flatten the body foil armature so it lies horizontal. Arrange the tail extending straight back for a resting position.
Fox family: scale the body armature down to 20 mm for fox kits beside the parent.
Needle tool fur texture: before curing, use the needle tool to draw short strokes all over the surface in the direction fur would grow, giving a more textured coat.

rows.fix: Keep ears at least 3 mm thick. Chill the assembled figure in the refrigerator for 10 minutes before transferring to the oven so thin parts firm up and hold their shape
rows.cause: Clay too warm and soft from handling, or ears too thin
rows.problem: Ear tips drooping or folding over before curing

rows.fix: Before curing, press the entire base of the figure against a flat tile and press down gently to ensure flat contact. Position the tail so its tip also contacts the tile
rows.cause: Base not flattened sufficiently, or tail contact point not bearing weight evenly
rows.problem: Figure rocking or unstable on its base

rows.fix: Work the boundary with short single strokes outward from the centre of each colour into the other, rather than back-and-forth mixing. Stop as soon as the transition looks gradual
rows.cause: Too much blending pressure or over-working the boundary
rows.problem: Colour blending leaving muddy brown zones instead of a clean gradient

rows.fix: Re-seat fallen beads with a small dab of jewellery adhesive. In future, press beads in until at least half their diameter is below the surface
rows.cause: Beads not pressed deeply enough into the clay before curing
rows.problem: Glass bead eyes falling out after curing
